source.title: Hierarchical nesting of slow oscillations, spindles and ripples in the human hippocampus during sleep

confidence_rationale: Canonical Nature Neuroscience paper providing direct intracranial EEG evidence from human patients for the hierarchical SO > spindle > ripple nesting. Spindles cluster ripples at spindle troughs -- the temporal framing mechanism that is MECH-122's content packaging function. Human data with cross-frequency coupling analysis, high methodological quality. Mapping fidelity high for the packaging function. Sensory gating function is not measured in this study.
failure_signatures: The nesting relationship (spindles cluster ripples) is correlational -- the study does not demonstrate that spindle disruption prevents ripple-timed content transfer, which is the causal claim embedded in MECH-122.

The hierarchy (SO > spindle > ripple) means spindles are themselves gated by slow oscillations; in REE the equivalent of the SO is not yet implemented in V3, making the spindle-equivalent mechanism only partially instantiated.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In human intracranial EEG during NREM sleep, sleep spindles are modulated by the up-state of cortical slow oscillations, and spindles in turn cluster hippocampal ripples at their troughs. This three-way hierarchical nesting provides fine-tuned temporal frames for the transfer of hippocampal memory traces to the neocortex.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-122 posits that spindle-equivalent bursts package E3/hippocampal replay (SWR-equivalent) content for z_theta delivery to E1 (neocortex-equivalent). Staresina et al's spindle-clusters-ripples mechanism IS the packaging function: the spindle burst temporally frames the SWR replay event, windows the z_theta transmission channel, and gates content delivery to the cortical surface. The ThetaBuffer already provides theta-rate packaging in V3 (MECH-089, EXQ-052b PASS); MECH-122 extends this to the reverse direction (E3 -> E1) during offline_consolidation mode, with the spindle-equivalent burst as the delivery window.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study measures coupling in human hippocampus during sleep but cannot directly test whether the spindle-coupled ripple content is selectively more likely to be consolidated than spindle-uncoupled ripples. The REE mapping also requires a slow-oscillation-equivalent mechanism to gate the spindle-equivalent bursts, which is not yet implemented. The z_theta delivery directionality (E3 -> E1 during sleep vs E1 -> E3 during waking) is a REE architectural claim not directly verified here.
mapping.source_context: Human epilepsy patients, intracranial EEG during natural sleep, hippocampus and neocortex, cross-frequency phase-amplitude coupling, 11 patients

confidence_components.notes: Nature Neuroscience, human intracranial EEG -- highest source quality for this domain. Mapping fidelity high for the packaging mechanism but the causal link between spindle-ripple nesting and content specificity of transfer is not shown. Transfer risk low: human data with direct hippocampal recordings.
